text: The selective, direct functionalization of methane into high-value chemicals remains a paramount challenge in modern chemistry. Currently, acetic acid is produced industrially via the methanol carbonylation process, which requires expensive homogeneous catalysts and poses significant environmental challenges. In this work, we present a novel photocatalytic pathway for the room-temperature synthesis of acetic acid from CH₄ and CO, utilizing water as the sole external oxygen source. Our optimized photocatalyst features a TiO₂ support decorated with ammonium phosphotungstic polyoxometalate (NPW) clusters and anchored with isolated platinum single atoms (Pt₁). This system achieves the stable production of a 5.7 mmol·L⁻¹ acetic acid solution over 60 hours, reaching a liquid-phase selectivity of over 90% and a carbon-basis selectivity of 66%, with a turnover of 99 moles of acetic acid per mole of platinum. Through a combination of isotopic labeling and in situ spectroscopy, we demonstrate that the reaction proceeds via the photocatalytic oxidative carbonylation of methane at the Pt₁ sites, with methane activation being significantly enhanced by water-derived hydroxyl radicals.
